questions.question: Why is a storage-platform interview drill more than just naming a KV store, cache, and blob store?
options: Because the real challenge is making the promises across those components internally consistent under scale and failure.
Because interviewers only care about vendor product names.
Because caches and metadata are the same thing.
Because durability never interacts with product requirements.
questions.explanation: The integration and trade-offs are where most of the signal lives.

questions.question: What should you usually clarify first in a broad storage-platform prompt?
options: Which user flows and constraints dominate the design such as upload, retrieval, listing, or retention.
The preferred slide color for the final summary.
Whether every bucket name should start with the same letter.
How many acronyms you are allowed to use.
questions.explanation: Without the dominant flows, architecture choices are mostly guesswork.

questions.question: Why is separating metadata and object planes so central in this drill?
options: Because query workloads and durable-byte workloads optimize for different systems and failure handling.
Because metadata cannot be versioned.
Because object storage can never support checksums.
Because listing APIs are always optional.
questions.explanation: The separation aligns architecture to access patterns and operational needs.

questions.question: Which metric most clearly reveals that the derived-asset cache is no longer protecting the platform well?
options: Cache hit rate dropping while origin or object-store read pressure rises sharply.
The number of people in the team chat.
Only total stored bytes.
The average length of object prefixes.
questions.explanation: Protection value is about offload, not cache existence by itself.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with offering multiple durability tiers in one platform?
options: Better business alignment on cost and safety, but more migration, explanation, and policy complexity.
No need for repair scheduling anymore.
Every customer automatically gets the strongest guarantee at no cost.
Lifecycle policies become unnecessary.
questions.explanation: Tiering helps fit the product, but it increases operational surface area.

questions.question: Why is upload finalize a natural deep dive in this phase?
options: It touches integrity, metadata correctness, retries, and orphan cleanup all at once.
It is only useful because it sounds sophisticated.
It removes the need for multipart upload state.
It guarantees cache hit rate improvements.
questions.explanation: Finalize is where durable bytes and control-plane truth meet.

questions.question: What is the strongest reason to include lifecycle and repair in a storage-platform answer?
options: Because storage reliability and cost depend on background maintenance, not just foreground APIs.
Because interviewers prefer longer answers.
Because background jobs are always free to run.
Because caches cannot be monitored directly.
questions.explanation: Ignoring maintenance creates incomplete or unrealistic designs.

questions.question: Which follow-up most strongly tests redesign skill?
options: Cost must drop 40% while compliance and legal-hold requirements become stricter.
The README title should be shorter.
One graph should be blue instead of green.
The object IDs should avoid numbers.
questions.explanation: That follow-up forces tiering, lifecycle, and architecture trade-offs to change together.
